summary: Soviet film director (1932–1986), known for long takes, philosophical themes, and visually poetic cinema. His major films are Solaris, Stalker, Andrei Rublev, Mirror, Nostalghia, and The Sacrifice. His father Arseny Tarkovsky was a poet whose verse appears as voiceover in several of his films. Wrote the book Sculpting in Time, advocating long takes over montage.

works.name: Solaris (1972)
works.description: Adaptation of Stanislaw Lem's novel about psychologist Kris Kelvin, who travels to a space station orbiting the ocean planet Solaris and encounters apparitions of his deceased wife Hari. A committee examines a man who claims to have seen a giant child's face in the ocean. Features close-ups of Brueghel's Hunters in the Snow. Ends with a reveal that Kelvin is on an island in the planet. Bondarchuk's daughter Natalya starred. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Psychologist Kris Kelvin travels to a space station orbiting the ocean planet Solaris and is visited by apparitions of his dead wife Hari; adapted from Stanislaw Lem's novel
examples: the psychologist Kris Kelvin travels to a space station where he is visited by apparitions of his deceased wife, Hari: that film is based on a novel by Stanislaw Lem
Kris begins to see his dead wife while investigating the death of one of three men orbiting the title water planet

clues.clue: A committee examines a man who claims to have seen the gigantic face of a child in the ocean
examples: That film by him includes a committee's examination of a man who claims to have seen the gigantic face of a child in the ocean.

clues.clue: Features close-ups of Brueghel's Hunters in the Snow; ends with a reveal that Kelvin is on an island in the planet
examples: Several close-ups of the painting also appear in this director's adaptation of Solaris.
The protagonist is troubled by hallucinations of his dead wife Hari in a film by this director, ending with the reveal that Kris Kelvin is on an island in the namesake planet.

works.name: Stalker (1979)
works.description: Adaptation of the Strugatsky brothers' novel Roadside Picnic. A Stalker guides a Writer and a Professor through the dangerous 'Zone' to a wish-granting Room. A three-man team passes through a tunnel called 'the meat grinder.' Porcupine, a previous guide, committed suicide after wishing for wealth instead of resurrecting his dead brother. A character puts a crown of thorns on his head after reaching the Room. The film's score features flute and tar evoking 'space frozen in dynamic equilibrium.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: A Stalker guides the Writer and the Professor through the Zone to a wish-granting Room; adapted from the Strugatsky brothers' Roadside Picnic
examples: a film about a professor and a writer journeying into the 'Zone.'
Name this Soviet director whose film Stalker follows three men through a disturbing tunnel known as 'the meat grinder' in a prohibited area known as 'the Zone.'

clues.clue: Porcupine, a former Stalker, is revealed to have committed suicide after wishing for wealth instead of resurrecting his dead brother
examples: Porcupine is revealed to have committed suicide after opting for wealth rather than resurrecting his dead brother in a film by this director

clues.clue: A character puts a crown of thorns on his head upon reaching the supposedly wish-granting Room, to much disappointment
examples: A character in a film by this director puts a crown of thorns on his head after reaching a supposedly desire-granting room to much disappointment.

clues.clue: The score features a theme for flute and tar evoking 'space frozen in a dynamic equilibrium'; the film transitions from sepia to color when men arrive at a field littered with abandoned military equipment
examples: A film by this director includes a theme for flute and tar meant to evoke 'space frozen in a dynamic equilibrium.' That film by this director transitions from sepia into full color when a group of men arrive at a field littered with abandoned military equipment.

works.name: Andrei Rublev (1966)
works.description: Historical epic about the life of 15th-century Russian icon painter Andrei Rublev. Divided into eight parts. Shot in black and white but ends with a color shot of horses grazing in a meadow. Features a scene of a horse falling down stairs and being impaled on a spear. Rublev is inspired to resume painting after watching a bell being cast and rung. Theophanes the Greek invites him to paint the Cathedral of the Annunciation. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Epic about 15th-century icon painter Andrei Rublev; divided into eight parts; shot in black-and-white but ends with a color shot of horses grazing; Rublev is inspired to resume painting after watching a bell being cast and rung
examples: A third film by this director is divided into eight parts and details the life of a 15th century iconographer.
the title Medieval artist is inspired to resume painting after seeing a bell being cast and rung.

clues.clue: Features a scene of a horse falling down a flight of stairs before being impaled on a spear
examples: In one film by this director, a horse is shown falling down a flight of stairs before being impaled on a spear.

clues.clue: Theophanes the Greek invites Rublev to paint the Cathedral of the Annunciation
examples: Theophanes the Greek invites the title character to paint the Cathedral of the Annunciation in one of his films.

works.name: Mirror (1975)
works.description: Autobiographical film told from the perspective of Alexei. His father Arseny Tarkovsky narrates his own poetry in voiceover. Features newsreel footage from the Spanish Civil War interspersed with Ignat reading a letter. Includes a 'levitation' scene and close-ups of Brueghel's Hunters in the Snow while JS Bach plays. A scene features a woman sitting on a well watching her family's barn burn down. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Autobiographical film; Arseny Tarkovsky (the director's father) narrates his own poetry in voiceover; includes a famous 'levitation' scene and close-ups of Brueghel's Hunters in the Snow while Bach plays
examples: This author narrates his poetry in the voiceover to a 1975 film featuring a shot in which a woman levitates over a bed.

works.name: Nostalghia (1983)
works.description: Made after Tarkovsky moved to Italy. Features a nine-minute-long take of a man attempting to walk across a dry swimming pool without extinguishing a candle. Ends with a man and dog lying in a ruined cathedral. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Features a nine-minute-long take of a man attempting to walk across a dry swimming pool without extinguishing a candle; ends with a man and dog lying in a ruined cathedral
examples: This director included a nine-minute-long take of a man attempting to walk across a dry swimming pool without extinguishing a candle towards the end of a film he made after moving to Italy.
A man and dog lay in a ruined cathedral in the last shot of this director's Nostalghia.

works.name: The Sacrifice (1986)
works.description: Tarkovsky's final film, made in Sweden. Ends with the words 'In the beginning was the Word' spoken by the otherwise silent 'Little Man.' Opens with Alexander planting a tree with his son in a nine-and-a-half minute tracking shot. Cinematography by Sven Nykvist. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Final film; ends with 'In the beginning was the Word' spoken by the silent 'Little Man'; opens with Alexander planting a tree with his son in a nine-and-a-half minute tracking shot; cinematography by Sven Nykvist
examples: This director's final film ends with the words 'in the beginning was the Word' being spoken by the otherwise silent 'Little Man.'
Another of his films opens with Alexander planting a tree with his son in a nine-and-a-half minute tracking shot characteristic of his later films.
Nykvist also served as cinematographer for this man's final film, The Sacrifice.

works.name: Ivan's Childhood (1962)
works.description: Tarkovsky's debut feature, a war film about a child scout for the Red Army. The film ends with the revelation that Ivan was captured and hanged by the Nazis. Influenced Sergei Parajanov to turn away from social realism toward vivid tableaux. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Debut feature about a child scout for the Red Army; ends with the revelation that Ivan was captured and hanged by the Nazis; influenced Parajanov to turn away from social realism
examples: This Soviet director's debut feature was the war film Ivan's Childhood.
it is revealed that Ivan was captured and hanged by the Nazis just before the end of the war

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Andrei Tarkovsky (1932–1986) was a Soviet film director. His father Arseny Tarkovsky was a poet whose verse appears as voiceover in Mirror and Stalker. Tarkovsky wrote the book Sculpting in Time, theorizing 'time pressure' of long takes in opposition to montage. Brueghel's Hunters in the Snow appears in multiple films (Solaris, Mirror). He was a key influence on Sergei Parajanov.

clues.clue: His father Arseny Tarkovsky was a poet whose verse appears as voiceover in Mirror and Stalker; Arseny had a romance with Marina Tsvetaeva
examples: name this author whose poetry is read in his son's films Mirror and Stalker.
This author narrates his poetry in the voiceover to a 1975 film

clues.clue: Wrote Sculpting in Time, theorizing 'time pressure' of long takes in opposition to Soviet montage; sided with Andre Bazin against montage
examples: This director sided with Bazin against montage, theorizing the 'time pressure' of long takes in his book Sculpting in Time.
The book Sculpting in Time discusses this author's poetry, including one read in a film by Porcupine's brother in the Zone.

clues.clue: Brueghel's Hunters in the Snow appears in both Solaris and Mirror; the diploma film from VGIK features a wrecking ball destroying a building
examples: His diploma-winning film from VGIK contains a famous shot of a wrecking ball destroying an old building.

Several close-ups of the painting also appear in this director's adaptation of Solaris. In a shot from this director's film The Mirror, a boy looks out at a frozen landscape that resembles Hunters in the Snow.

comprehensive_summary:
Andrei Tarkovsky (1932–1986) was a Soviet film director known for slow, contemplative, visually poetic filmmaking. His father Arseny Tarkovsky was a poet whose verse appears as voiceover in both Mirror and Stalker; Arseny had a romantic relationship with Marina Tsvetaeva. Tarkovsky wrote the book Sculpting in Time, in which he theorized the 'time pressure' of long takes in opposition to Soviet montage theory, siding with critic André Bazin.

Solaris (1972) is his most frequently clued film. Adapted from Stanislaw Lem's novel, it follows psychologist Kris Kelvin, who travels to a space station orbiting the ocean planet Solaris and is visited by apparitions of his dead wife Hari. A committee examines a man who claims to have seen a giant child's face in the ocean. The film features close-ups of Brueghel's Hunters in the Snow and ends with the revelation that Kelvin is actually on an island in the planet. Bondarchuk's daughter Natalya starred.

Stalker (1979), adapted from the Strugatsky brothers' Roadside Picnic, follows a Stalker who guides a Writer and a Professor through the dangerous Zone (entering through a tunnel called 'the meat grinder') to a supposedly wish-granting Room. Porcupine, a former Stalker, committed suicide after wishing for wealth instead of resurrecting his dead brother. A character puts a crown of thorns on his head upon reaching the Room. The film's score features a theme for flute and tar evoking 'space frozen in a dynamic equilibrium'; it transitions from sepia to color when the men arrive at a field littered with abandoned military equipment.

Andrei Rublev (1966) is a historical epic about the 15th-century Russian icon painter, divided into eight parts. Shot in black and white, it ends with a color shot of horses grazing in a meadow. A horse falls down stairs and is impaled on a spear in one scene. Rublev is inspired to resume painting after watching a bell being cast and rung; Theophanes the Greek invites him to paint the Cathedral of the Annunciation.

Mirror (1975) is an autobiographical film from the perspective of Alexei; Arseny Tarkovsky narrates his own poetry in voiceover. It features a 'levitation' scene and close-ups of Brueghel's Hunters in the Snow while JS Bach plays; newsreel footage from the Spanish Civil War is interspersed with a scene of Ignat reading a letter; a woman sits on a well watching her family's barn burn. Nostalghia (1983), made after Tarkovsky moved to Italy, includes a nine-minute-long take of a man crossing a dry swimming pool without extinguishing a candle; it ends with a man and dog in a ruined cathedral. The Sacrifice (1986), his final film (made in Sweden), ends with 'In the beginning was the Word' spoken by the silent 'Little Man'; it opens with Alexander planting a tree with his son in a nine-and-a-half minute tracking shot; cinematography by Sven Nykvist. Ivan's Childhood (1962), his debut, follows a child scout for the Red Army and ends with the revelation that Ivan was captured and hanged by the Nazis; it influenced Sergei Parajanov's shift away from social realism.
recursive_suggestions: Stalker (1979 film) — distinct from the novel Roadside Picnic; specific clues about the Zone, the Room, Porcupine (Fine Arts)
Solaris (1972 film) — check if has own entry separate from the Lem novel (Fine Arts)
Andrei Rublev (film) — may have own detailed entry (Fine Arts)
Arseny Tarkovsky — appears as its own answerline clue (Literature)
